record_office_terminology.probate: Probate Court (has jurisdiction over wills, estates, marriages, adoptions, and guardianships)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Probate Court (county-level marriage licenses from county formation; statewide registration 1949)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1908 (births/deaths statewide registration; some cities kept earlier records — Cincinnati from 1874, Cleveland from 1870s)

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Ohio Burned Counties; Ohio History Connection county records guides; county-level histories and genealogical society publications. Ohio has very few courthouse fire losses compared to Southern states.
administrative_notes: Ohio was the 17th state, admitted March 1, 1803 from the Northwest Territory (though Congress did not formally ratify until August 7, 1953 due to a technicality). The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 prohibited slavery in the territory. Ohio's land was surveyed under multiple systems: the Virginia Military District (south-central, between the Scioto and Little Miami Rivers — metes and bounds), the Connecticut Western Reserve (northeastern Ohio — Connecticut settlers), the U.S. Military District (Congress Lands — rectangular survey), the Ohio Company Purchase (southeastern), and the Symmes Purchase (southwestern). The first permanent American settlement was Marietta (1788). State capitals: Chillicothe (1803-1810, 1812-1816), Zanesville (1810-1812), Columbus (1816-present). Ohio was a major destination for westward migration from New England, Virginia, Pennsylvania, and Europe.

core_repositories.source_name: Ohio History Connection (formerly Ohio Historical Society)
core_repositories.description: Primary state repository for Ohio. Houses county records on microfilm (deeds, marriages, wills, probate, tax records), state government records, military records, canal records, and manuscript collections. Ohio Memory digital gateway provides online access to selected digitized collections. The Archives/Library Research Center in Columbus is essential for Ohio research.

core_repositories.repository: Ohio History Connection / State Library of Ohio
core_repositories.description: Ohio's free digital gateway to photographs, documents, maps, and other primary sources from Ohio's archives, libraries, museums, and historical societies. Includes digitized county records, photographs, and manuscripts.

Major research center for northeastern Ohio (Connecticut Western Reserve). Holds Connecticut Land Company records, Western Reserve land records, county histories, family papers, and extensive manuscript collections. Research Library in Cleveland.

Major research center for southwestern Ohio and the Cincinnati metropolitan area. Holds extensive collections of family papers, business records, organizational records, maps, and photographs. Symmes Purchase records, German immigrant community records, and Underground Railroad documentation.

core_repositories.source_name: State Library of Ohio — Genealogy Services
core_repositories.description: Maintains Ohio county histories, family histories, DAR records, and genealogical periodicals. Access to Ancestry Library Edition and other databases. Part of the SEO (SearchOhio) consortium providing access to local library genealogy collections statewide.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, probate, court), vital records, cemetery records, military records, church records, and naturalization records. Extensive microfilm partnerships with Ohio counties and Ohio History Connection provide digitized county-level records.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, land district information (VMD, Western Reserve, Congress Lands), and links to digitized microfilm. Includes Ohio burned counties list and research strategies.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Ohio newspapers from the 1800s forward. Includes major papers (Cincinnati Enquirer, Cleveland Plain Dealer, Columbus Dispatch) and hundreds of smaller county weeklies. Legal notices, obituaries, marriage announcements, and estate proceedings are valuable genealogical sources.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Ohio History Connection / State Library of Ohio
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Additional digitized Ohio newspapers not in Chronicling America. Includes many small-town county weeklies that document legal notices, estates, marriage announcements, and community events across rural Ohio.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: County Courts of Common Pleas / NARA Chicago / FamilySearch
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Ohio was a major immigration destination. Pre-1906 naturalizations filed at any court of record — county Court of Common Pleas, city courts, and federal courts. Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Records include declarations of intention ('first papers') and petitions for naturalization ('second papers'). Major immigrant groups: German (largest), Irish, Welsh, English, Scottish, Italian, Hungarian, Slovak, Czech, Polish, Slovenian, Croatian, Jewish, Greek, and Lebanese.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Ohio Ethnic Newspapers and Fraternal Society Records
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Various — Western Reserve Historical Society, Ohio History Connection, ethnic heritage societies
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Ethnic newspapers published in German, Hungarian, Italian, Czech, Slovak, and other languages contain vital event notices. Fraternal organization records (e.g., German Verein, Sons of Italy, Polish National Alliance, Sokol) document members and their families. German-language newspapers were especially numerous in Cincinnati, Columbus, and Cleveland.

military_records.description: Civil War records: Ohio Roster Commission compiled the 'Official Roster of the Soldiers of the State of Ohio in the War of the Rebellion' (12 volumes, ~320,000 soldiers). Also holds War of 1812 records, Indian War records (1790s), Mexican War records, and WWI/WWII records. Ohio Adjutant General records. Soldiers' and Sailors' Home records (Sandusky, Dayton).

military_records.source_name: Ohio Civil War — Roster Commission Records
military_records.description: The Ohio Roster Commission (established 1886) compiled service records for Ohio's approximately 320,000 Civil War soldiers. Published in 12 volumes. Includes unit histories, enlistment/discharge dates, and casualty information. Cross-reference with NARA pension files and compiled service records.

The Central Branch National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers in Dayton (established 1867) was one of the first veterans' homes. Records include admission registers, medical records, and death records for thousands of Civil War veterans and later conflicts.

land_and_property.repository: Ohio History Connection / Library of Virginia / BLM-GLO
land_and_property.description: The Virginia Military District (between the Scioto and Little Miami Rivers) was reserved for Virginia Revolutionary War bounty land warrants. Unlike other Ohio lands, it used metes and bounds survey. Records at Virginia State Library, Ohio History Connection, and BLM-GLO (federal patents). Approximately 14,000 warrants issued from 1784 to 1871.

land_and_property.source_name: Connecticut Western Reserve / Connecticut Land Company Records
land_and_property.repository: Western Reserve Historical Society, Cleveland / CT State Library
land_and_property.description: The Connecticut Western Reserve (northeastern Ohio, roughly 3.3 million acres) was held by Connecticut until 1800. Connecticut Land Company purchased most of the land in 1795. Records document original purchasers and settlers, many from Connecticut and other New England states. Firelands (Sufferers' Lands) in Erie and Huron Counties were granted to Connecticut residents burned out by the British.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Ohio
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Ohio's Congress Lands (rectangular survey system). Ohio was the first state surveyed under the federal system. Original entry case files include applications, certificates, and plats. The Seven Ranges (first federal survey, 1785), U.S. Military District, Refugee Tract, and other federal land districts are documented.

vital_records.source_name: Ohio Department of Health — Bureau of Vital Statistics
vital_records.description: State vital records: births and deaths from December 20, 1908 (statewide registration). For earlier events, check county probate court (marriages from county formation), city health department records (Cincinnati births/deaths from 1874, Cleveland from 1870s, Columbus from 1870s), church records, and cemetery records. Birth records over 100 years old available to the public.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Various — Ohio History Connection, denominational archives, FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are essential for Ohio genealogy, especially for pre-1908 vital events. Major denominations: Methodist (strongest in Ohio), Baptist, Presbyterian, Congregational (Western Reserve), Quaker (eastern and southwestern Ohio), Lutheran (German communities), Catholic (Cincinnati archdiocese from 1821, Cleveland diocese from 1847), Amish/Mennonite (Holmes, Wayne, Geauga counties), Moravian (Tuscarawas County), and numerous smaller denominations. Many records microfilmed by FamilySearch.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Ohio's 88 counties. Ohio has thousands of small rural and church cemeteries in addition to municipal cemeteries. Many Ohio genealogical societies have published cemetery transcription volumes. OGS (Ohio Genealogical Society) chapters have done extensive cemetery documentation.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society headquartered in Bellville (Richland County). Publishes The Ohio Genealogical Society Quarterly and The Report. Maintains an extensive library, surname file index, and county chapter network covering all 88 counties. Annual conferences. OGS chapters at the county level are invaluable for local research.

Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, obituary lookups, historical sketches, Bible records, county histories, and county-specific research guides for all 88 Ohio counties.

Online encyclopedia of Ohio history including county histories, biographical entries, and cultural topics. Useful for understanding county formation, settlement patterns, and historical context for genealogical research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Ohio has no abolished or extinct counties currently. All 88 counties exist today. However, Ohio's county boundaries changed significantly in the early 19th century as new counties were carved from existing ones. The original Northwest Territory counties in Ohio were Washington (1788), Hamilton (1790), Wayne (1796), Adams (1797), Jefferson (1797), and Ross (1798). From these, all 88 modern counties were eventually created. The last county formed was Noble County (1851). Researchers should carefully trace parent county chains since records before a county's formation year are in the parent county. Additionally, the 'Toledo War' (1835) boundary dispute between Ohio and Michigan resulted in the Toledo Strip being awarded to Ohio (Lucas County) — some early records may be in Michigan.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Ohio has remarkably few courthouse fire losses compared to Southern and border states. The only county with documented significant courthouse fire losses is Paulding County (1871 fire destroyed some early deed and court records). Other counties may have had minor records losses from flooding (especially the 1913 Great Flood which devastated Dayton and other Miami River valley communities) or localized incidents, but these did not result in catastrophic county-wide record loss.
